content:
# Titus Chapter 2 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 222-223 | **Source Images:** source-texts/greek-NT/TIT_001_page_001.jpg, source-texts/greek-NT/TIT_002_page_001.jpg

---

**1** But you, speak the things which are fitting to the sound teaching.

**2** Old men to be sober-minded, dignified, self-controlled, being-sound in the faith, in the love, in the endurance.

**3** Old women likewise in deportment reverent, not slanderers, not having-been-enslaved to much wine, teachers-of-what-is-good,

**4** so-that they-might-train-the-young-women to-be lovers-of-[their]-husbands, lovers-of-[their]-children,

**5** self-controlled, pure, workers-at-home, good, being-submitted to their own husbands, so-that the word of God might not be blasphemed.

**6** The younger [men] likewise exhort to-be self-controlled,

**7** concerning all things presenting yourself [as] a pattern of good works, in the teaching [showing] incorruptness, dignity,

**8** sound speech [that is] beyond-reproach, so-that the [one] from the opposing [side] might-be-ashamed, having nothing evil to say concerning us.

**9** Slaves to their own masters to-be-submitted in all things, to-be well-pleasing, not speaking-against,

**10** not pilfering, but showing all good faithfulness, so-that they-might-adorn the teaching, the [teaching] of the Savior of-us, God, in all things.

**11** For the grace of God has-appeared, saving to all men,

**12** training us, so-that having-denied the ungodliness and the worldly desires, self-controlledly and righteously and godly we-might-live in the present age,

**13** awaiting the blessed hope and appearing of the glory of the great God and Savior of-us, Christ Jesus,

**14** who gave himself on-behalf-of us, so-that he-might-redeem us from all lawlessness and might-purify for-himself a people [for his] own-possession, zealous of good works.

**15** These things speak and exhort and reprove with all authority. Let no one despise you.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **Verse 3 — ΚΑΛΟΔΙΔΑΣΚΑΛΟΥΣ (kalodidaskalous):** This is a compound word unique to this passage, rendered "teachers-of-what-is-good." The Sinaiticus text preserves this as a single compound adjective in the accusative plural.

2. **Verse 5 — ΟΙΚΟΥΡΓΟΥΣ (oikourgous):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΙΚΟΥΡΓΟΥΣ ("workers-at-home") rather than the variant ΟΙΚΟΥΡΟΥΣ ("keepers-of-the-home") found in some later manuscripts. The Sinaiticus reading emphasizes active domestic labor rather than merely staying at home.

3. **Verse 7 — ΑΦΘΟΡΙΑΝ (aphthorian):** Sinaiticus reads "incorruptness" (ΑΦΘΟΡΙΑΝ). Some manuscripts add ΣΕΜΝΟΤΗΤΑ ("dignity") as a separate quality; Sinaiticus includes it as part of the same construction.

4. **Verse 8 — ΗΜΩΝ (hemon) vs. ΥΜΩΝ (humon):** Sinaiticus reads "concerning us" (ΠΕΡΙ ΗΜΩΝ) rather than "concerning you" (ΠΕΡΙ ΥΜΩΝ) as found in some later manuscripts. This is a significant textual variant affecting whether Paul includes himself with Titus.

5. **Verse 11 — ΣΩΤΗΡΙΟΣ (soterios):** The adjective "saving" modifies "grace" — rendered literally as "the grace of God has-appeared, saving to all men." Some translations restructure this to make "salvation" a noun, but the Greek construction places ΣΩΤΗΡΙΟΣ as an adjective modifying the grace. Sinaiticus preserves the adjectival form.

6. **Verse 13 — ΤΟΥ ΜΕΓΑΛΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ΚΑΙ ΣΩΤΗΡΟΣ ΗΜΩΝ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΥ ΙΗΣΟΥ:** The single article ΤΟΥ governing both "great God" and "Savior" in Sinaiticus grammatically identifies Christ Jesus as both "the great God" and "Savior" (Granville Sharp's rule). This is preserved literally without theological smoothing.

7. **Verse 14 — ΛΑΟΝ ΠΕΡΙΟΥΣΙΟΝ (laon periousion):** Rendered "a people [for his] own-possession." ΠΕΡΙΟΥΣΙΟΝ echoes the LXX designation of Israel as God's treasured possession (cf. Exodus 19:5, Deuteronomy 14:2). The word carries the sense of a specially acquired, prized people.

8. **General note on scriptio continua:** Codex Sinaiticus is written in continuous script without word divisions. All word-boundary decisions in this translation reflect standard scholarly parsing of the uncial text as visible on pages 222-223 of the manuscript.

--- EMET
